coredata.dc:title: Surface chemical engineering towards efficient and bright chemiluminescent carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Cheng-Long
Zang, Jin-Hao
Lou, Qing
Zheng, Guang-Song
Wu, Meng-Yuan
Ye, Yang-Li
Zhu, Jin-Yang
Liu, Kai-Kai
Dong, Lin
Shan, Chong-Xin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (CDs) are emerging as low toxic chemiluminescent (CL) materials for new-generation cold lighting and bioimaging technology due to their tunable and efficient emissions. Nevertheless, continuous progress and efforts are still needed to improve the luminance and lifetime of the CL CDs. Here, CL quenching is observed in the CD-peroxyoxalate-hydrogen peroxide system, resulting from the water-induced aggregation and blocking effect of the electron exchange between the CDs and energetic intermediate produced in the CL reaction. A surface chemical regulation strategy is first introduced to improve the CL performance of CDs. Optimized the capped ligand species and chain length, the CDs modified by n-butylamine exhibit a CL quantum yield of 1.15 × 10–2 einsteins mol−1 and maximal luminance of 5.11 cd m−2, which is about 5- and 13-fold higher than the initial CDs and comparable with commercially available Rhodamine B. The enhanced performance can be explained by the decreased electron capture from water molecules generated due to the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide in the CL process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Chemiluminescence
Luminescence quenching
Surface chemical engineering
Electron exchange

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and surface chemical engineering of the CDs
The measurement of CL spectrum and CL luminance

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the support of this research by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U2004168, 12074348, 12004345, U1804155, and U1604263), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M682310), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (212300410078) and the Provincial and Ministerial Co-construction of Collaborative Innovation Center Foundation for Resource Materials (zycl202006).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM images of the CDs by depositing the CDs ethanol drops on the carbon-coated copper grids (inset: the size distribution of the CDs). (b) XRD pattern of the CDs. (c) The full XPS survey spectra of the CDs. (d) XPS C1s spectra of the CDs. (e) XPS N1s spectra of the CDs.

(a) Photographs of the CDs dispersed in different solution under sunlight and 365-nm UV lamp. (b) The excitation, emission and UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs in aqueous solution. (c) Time-resolved decay spectra of the CDs aqueous solution (inset: the same decay curves with the fitted results in a semilog scale and schematic illustration of the PL process). (d) Photographs of the CDs-based CL by adding 4-mL H2O2 solution (containing 0.16-mL 30% H2O2 aqueous solution) into the mixture of 0.1-g mL−1 CPPO ethyl acetate solution (1 mL) and 3-mg mL−1 CDs ethanol solution (1 mL). (e) The CL intensity of these CDs-based CL compared by CL analysis system. (f) The CL spectrum of corresponding CD-based CL. (g) The CL spectrum triggered by H2O2 ethanol solution with different adding order. (h) The CL spectrum measured after adding H2O2 ethanol solution into the mixture of CPPO and CDs. (i) the CL signal curve triggered by different concentration of CDs (insets: the fitted curve of CL intensity vs. Concentration).

(a) The light emission spectrum of the CDs-based CL before and after adding water. (b) The CL intensity after adding H2O2 ethanol solution or aqueous solution into the mixture of CPPO and CDs. (c) The CL signal curve induced by the H2O2 ethanol solution containing different concentration of water (inset is the fitted curve). (d) The TEM image by depositing the CDs water drops on the carbon-coated copper grids. (e) The DLS of CDs aqueous solution. (f) The PL lifetime of CDs solution ethanol solution containing different concentration of water (inset: the same decay curves with the fitted results in a semilog scale). (g) Schematic illustration of the process of CIEEL in the CD-based CL. (h) Schematic illustration of the ET between different CDs triggered by water.

(a) Schematic illustration of the surface chemical route to modify the CDs with CTAB, ODA and OA. (b) The distribution of initial CDs and CDs modified with CTAB, ODA and OA in H2O/DCM (up is water and bottom is DCM). (c) The FT-IR spectrum of initial CDs and three kinds of CDs. (d) The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the initial CDs in ethanol and three kinds of CDs in DCM. (e) The normalized CL spectrum of initial CDs and three kinds of CDs. (f) The CL intensity after adding H2O2 ethanol solution into the mixture of CPPO and different CDs. (g) The CL intensity after adding H2O2 aqueous solution into the mixture of CPPO and different CDs. (h) The calculated CL QY of different CDs triggered by H2O2 ethanol solution or aqueous solution.

(a) Schematic illustration of the surface chemical route to modify the CDs with different long-chain amino surfactants. (b) The distribution of initial CDs and CDs modified with NBA, NTA, ODA and PbN (from left to right) in H2O/DCM (up is water and bottom is DCM). (c) The FT-IR spectrum of initial CDs and three kinds of CDs. (d) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of different modified CDs. (e) The normalized CL spectrum after adding H2O2 ethanol solution into the mixture of CPPO and three kinds of CDs. (f) The CL intensity decay after adding H2O2 ethanol solution into the mixture of CPPO and three kinds of CDs. (g) The calculated CL QY of different CDs triggered by H2O2 ethanol solution. (i) The CL QYs of CDs as a function of length of chain (the number of alkyl C).

(a) The CL photograph after injecting H2O2 into the mixture of CPPO and CDs@NBA or RhB. (b) The normalized CL spectrum of the illumination with CDs@NBA or RhB as chemiluminescent emitter. (c) The luminescence intensity after adding after injecting H2O2 into the mixture of CPPO and different chemiluminescent emitter. (d) The maximal CL intensity of these illumination triggered by different chemiluminescent dyes. (e) The integrated luminescence efficiency of the CDs modified with different surfactants compared with RhB.
The CL property of different chemiluminescent emitters in illumination.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a)the maximal CL wavelength; b)the CIE of the CL induced by different chemiluminescent dyes; c)CL quantum yield; d)CL maximum intensity.

head.ce:title: Surface chemical engineering towards efficient and bright chemiluminescent carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Diamond Optoelectronic Materials and Devices, Key Laboratory of Material Physics, Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450052, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Centre for International Cooperation on Designer Low-carbon & Environmental Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Centre for International Cooperation on Designer Low-carbon & Environmental Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Centre for International Cooperation on Designer Low-carbon & Environmental Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots (CDs) are emerging as low toxic chemiluminescent (CL) materials for new-generation cold lighting and bioimaging technology due to their tunable and efficient emissions. Nevertheless, continuous progress and efforts are still needed to improve the luminance and lifetime of the CL CDs. Here, CL quenching is observed in the CD-peroxyoxalate-hydrogen peroxide system, resulting from the water-induced aggregation and blocking effect of the electron exchange between the CDs and energetic intermediate produced in the CL reaction. A surface chemical regulation strategy is first introduced to improve the CL performance of CDs. Optimized the capped ligand species and chain length, the CDs modified by n-butylamine exhibit a CL quantum yield of 1.15 × 10–2 einsteins mol−1 and maximal luminance of 5.11 cd m−2, which is about 5- and 13-fold higher than the initial CDs and comparable with commercially available Rhodamine B. The enhanced performance can be explained by the decreased electron capture from water molecules generated due to the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide in the CL process.

ce:section.ce:para: Light emission induced by chemical reaction, defined as chemiluminescence (CL), has evoked considerable interest in ultrasensitive chemical analysis, cold illuminate and bioimaging for many years, due to its special light emission process and no background light interference [1–14]. With the advance in chemical synthesis, many high-efficiency and long-lifetime CL systems like peroxalate-hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), luminol-H2O2 and lucigenin-H2O2 have been developed to chemically excite various emitters and then produce multicolor light [4,5,7,15]. Originally, the luminophors in these CL reactions are limited in small-molecular dyes, such as Rhodamine B (RhB), Rubrene, Cyanine, etc. [10,16,17]. However, the serious obstacles like potential biotoxicity, low photostability, and poor biocompatibility seriously limit their applications. To overcome these tough issues, other alternative nanomaterials including metal nanoparticles, colloidal quantum dots (QDs), semiconductor polymer nanoparticles and aggregation-induced-emission nanoparticles have been developed to be employed as emitter in CL reactions [7,8,18–22] . Nevertheless, the problems of low luminance or inherent potential biotoxicity of these nanomaterials is still a challenge for their practical application.

Fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs), as one new class of promising candidate with excellent properties like high fluorescence quantum yield (QY), tunable photoluminescence (PL) wavelength, good biocompatibility, high photo-stability, have been widely developed in light-emission-diode (LED), laser and bioimaging, etc. [23–39] . Meanwhile, with the unique ability of luminous emission, CDs have exhibited tremendous potential as emitting species in some CL systems like lucigenin-H2O2, luminol-H2O2 and luminol-KMnO4, etc. [40–44] . Besides, our prior works have achieved multicolor CDs-based CL emission in peroxalate-H2O2 system, whose CL efficiency can be boosted by improving the chemically induced electron exchange (CIEE) between the CDs and high-energy intermediate produced in the luminescence process [12,13,45] . By adjusting the degree of graphitization and the size of the sp2 domains in CDs, a proper energy level alignment between the CDs and the energy-rich intermediate can promote the electron transfer between the CDs and the energy-rich intermediate. Analogy with controlling the basic structure of CDs, surface chemical engineering of CDs through ligand exchange is deduced to another approach to improve the electron transfer between the CDs and the intermediate, which has been widely demonstrated their potential important role in high-performance electroluminescent devices and high solvent-adaptability luminescent marker in different-polarity media. Hence, it is still an imperious demand to investigate the influence factor of surface chemical modulation on the CL emission of CDs and further develop more efficient CL CDs.

In this work, one kind of water-soluble CDs with green emission has been used as lumiphore in the CL reaction of bis(2,4,5-trichloro-6-carbopentoxyphenyl) oxalate (CPPO) and H2O2. It is found the performance of the CD-based CL is influenced by the solvent environment and especially can be quenched by water media, which can be attributed to that water can inhibit the electron exchange between the CDs and energetic intermediate produced in the CL reaction due to the water-induced aggregation and strong energy transfer (ET) between different CDs. A surface chemical engineering route has been first attempted to decrease the influence of interfacial ET process between CDs and improve the CL performance of CDs. With the optimal selection of the capped species and chain length of different surface surfactants, it is found that the CDs modified by n-butylamine (NBA) exhibit a maximal CL QY of 1.15 × 10–2 einsteins mol−1, which is 5-fold higher than the initial CDs. Meanwhile, the CDs can rival the CL luminance of commercially available RhB dyes, showing a maximal brightness of 5.11 cd m−2. This work reveals the importance of the surface radical in the process of CL and paves a facile route to design efficient chemiluminescent CDs for practical applications.

Bis(2,4,5-trichloro-6-carbopentoxyphenyl) peroxalate (CPPO, 98%), citric acid (C6H8O7, CA, 99.9%), urea (CON2H4, 99.9%), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.9%), N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMAC, 99.9%), Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium Bromide (CTAB, 99%), octadecylamine (C18H39N, ODA, 97%), oleic acid (C18H34O2, OA, AR), n-butylamine (C4H11N, NBA 99.5%), n-tetradecylamine (C14H31N, NTA, 96%), Poly(propylene glycol) bis(2-aminopropyl ether) (CH3(NH2)CH2[OCH2CH(CH3)]nNH2, PbN, average ~ 2000), Rhodamine B (C28H31ClN2O3, RhB, AR) were purchased by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, P. R. China). methyl alcohol (MeOH, 99.5%), ethyl alcohol (EtOH, 99.5%), tetrahydrofuran (THF, 99.5%), dichloromethane (DCM, 99.5%), ethyl acetate (EAC, 99.9%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), dibutyl phthalate (DBP, 99.9%) and tert-butanol (C4H10O, TBA, 99.0%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, P. R. China). The chemicals reagents were analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

The surface morphology was characterized by a field emission transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL JSM-IT100). The X-ray diffractometer (Panalytical X’ Pert Pro) using Cu kα as the irradiation source was used to obtain the XRD patterns. The Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was recorded on a Bio-Rad Excalibur spectrometer (Bruker vector 22). Hydrogen nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra of the CDs were achieved on a Varian mercury-plus 400 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded used Kratos AXIS HIS 165 spectrometer with a monochromatized Al KR X-ray source (1486.7 eV). The UV–Vis absorption spectra were collected using a Shimadzu UV-3101PC spectrometer. The fluorescence excitation-emission matrixes were measure with a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer. The fluorescence decay curves were measured by Horiba FL-322 using a 370 nm Nano-LED monitoring the related emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and surface chemical engineering of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 1-g citric acid and 2-g urea were dispersed in 10-mL DMAC. The mixtures were added into Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (20 mL). Then the sealed autoclave vessels were placed into an electric oven which was set at 160 °C for 8 h. After reaction, the solvents were dispersed in absolute ethyl alcohol and treated with 8000 rpm min−1 centrifugation obtain the precipitation. After three repetitions, the precipitation was collected and dried at 60 °C for 1 day for characterizations and further used.

The CDs were modified with different surfactants by corresponding chemical grafting routes: 0.2-g CDs were dispersed into 50-mL DMF, then different surfactants (0.8-g CTAB, 0.8-g ODA, 2-mL OA, 2-mL NBA, 0.8-g NTA or 2-mL PbN were added into the solution. After stirring in the atmosphere for 12 h under 80 °C, the solution was evaporated to dryness by rotary evaporator under 80 °C. The as-prepared CDs were redispersed into ethyl acetate and treated with 3000 rpm min−1 centrifugation to remove the precipitation. The final CDs were dried using a vacuum drying oven and collected for further characterizations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The measurement of CL spectrum and CL luminance
ce:section.ce:para: The CL spectrum was measured by the F-7000 spectrophotometer with the lamp off. The CL intensity measurement was carried out with a laboratory-built flow injection CL system, which is consisted of an IFFM-E flow injection system (Ruimai Electronic Science Co., China), Photomultiplier tube (PMT), IFFS-A luminometer (Ruimai Electronic Science Co., China) and a common computer. The CL signals were monitored by the PMT adjacent to the flow CL cell. The relationship between CL signals and the concentration of three reactants was measured in this work. 30% H2O2 was dispersed in ethyl alcohol for different concentrations (1, 0.25, 0.125, 0.0625, 0.03125 and 0.015625 M). CPPO was dispersed into ethyl acetate for different concentrations (2, 1, 0.5, 0.25 and 0.125 mM). CDs were dispersed into ethyl alcohol for different concentrations (3, 1.5, 0.75, 0.325 and 0.1526 mg mL−1). Three flow lines were used for CL flow-injection system. CPPO was firstly mixed with CD and H2O2 was then mixed with the mixture in the cell. With different concentrations of reactant, the CL intensity signals were recorded by the PMT.

The CL QYs of CDs were measured using lucigenin-H2O2 as a reference CL system with a known CL QY of 11.3 × 10–3 einsteins mol−1 at pH = 13.8 according to the previous literatures [12,13,18] . With the CL spectra and kinetic curves, the CL QYs were calculated according to the following equations:
(1)
ϕ
CL
=
Q ×
f
luc
×
f
photo
n
( e i n s t e i n s / m o l )
(2)
f
luc
=
ϕ
luc
×
n
luc
Q
luc
(3)
f
photo
=
f (
λ s
)
f (
λ
luc
)
where ϕCL is the CL QY of the CDs, Q is the total light emission obtained by integration of emission intensity under time curves. In the equation, fluc is obtained by measuring the emission kinetics of lucigenin reaction performed in standard conditions (λ = 530 nm). fphoto is obtained from the sensitivity of CL spectrum normalized at the emission wavelength (λ = 530 nm) of the lucigenin standard, f(λluc), and at the emission wavelength (λ = 530 nm) of the CDs, f(λCD). n is the number of moles of lucigenin (nluc) or the number of moles of CPPO.

The green chemiluminescent CDs are prepared with citric (1 g) and urea (2 g) reacted in dimethylacetamide (DMAC) via a solvothermal strategy as illustrated in Experiment section. As shown in Fig. 1 a, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is employed to investigate the morphology of the CDs by depositing the CD ethanol solution (0.3 mg mL−1) on the carbon-coated copper grids. The CDs display a size distribution ranging from 2 to 10 nm with an average diameter of 4.8 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image reveals the CDs with a well-resolved lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystallographic plane of graphitic carbon. The X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD), as illustrated in Fig. 2 b, exhibits a peak at 22.8° attributed to the graphitic structure with spacing (001), corresponding to the HRTEM results. The survey XPS spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 2c) exhibit the composition of C (284.0 eV), N (400.0 eV), and O (532.4 eV) elements. The high-resolution XPS C1s and N1s spectra can be found in Fig. 2d and 2e. For the CDs, the high-resolution XPS C1s envelope can be deconvoluted into three Gaussian peaks corresponding to sp2 C (CC/CC) at 284.2 eV, sp3 C (CN) at 285.2 eV, and (CO)O at 287.6 eV. And the N1s envelope can be deconvoluted into two Gaussian peaks corresponding to amino N at 398.9 eV and pyrrolic N at 399.5 eV. Meanwhile, the O1s envelope can be deconvoluted into two Gaussian peaks corresponding to quinone O at 530.7 eV and carbonyl O at 531.6 eV (Fig. S1). The 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra (Fig. S2) illustrate the obvious signals of aromatic H, COOH, NH2 in the CDs. All the characterization indicates the CDs possess the structure of inner sp2 C core grafted with diversified polar groups on their surface.

As illustrated in Fig. 2a, the CDs can be well dispersed in polar solvents like water, DMF, DMAC, MeOH, EtOH and THF due to the existence of their polar surface. These CD solutions can emit bright green fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp and illustrate a slight red shift in less polar solvents in comparison with water due to the dipole − dipole and hydrogen bonding interactions (Fig. S3). The PL property of CDs in aqueous solution is further investigated, exhibiting a maximal emission wavelength around 530 nm under the excitation wavelength of 460 nm (Fig. 2b). Meanwhile, the UV − Vis absorption spectrum of the CDs presents two different strong absorption band around 360 nm and 420 nm, implying the transition of C core contributed π → π* and surface state contributed n → π*. Furthermore, the PL quantum yield (PL QY) of the CDs aqueous solution are 33% and the time-resolved decay spectrum of the CDs in aqueous solution excited by 370 nm can be well-fitted by one-order exponentially decay with a lifetime of 6.64 ns (Fig. 2c). On the basis of the above results, the PL mechanism is proposed (Inset of Fig. 2c). First, the exciting light undergo a strong resonant absorption through the π → π* transition of sp2 C cores and the n → π* transition of surface N-O states. Then, the photoexcited electron migrates from the crystalline core to surface groups and generate a radiative recombination with the holes left in the valence band with a bright fluorescence.

With the excellent PL property, the CDs are used as one new class of CL fluorophores in CPPO-H2O2 reaction. Here, we found the CDs show a solvent-dependent CL behavior, whose intensity is relied on the solvent types for CL reaction. As shown in Fig. 2d, green CL emission with different intensities can be observed after adding 4-mL different kinds of solvents (containing 0.16-mL 30% H2O2 aqueous solution) into the mixture of CPPO ethyl acetate solution (1 mL, 0.1 g mL−1) and CDs ethanol solution (1 mL, 3 mg mL−1). In these solution systems, the CL after adding H2O2/EtOH mixed solvent is brightest, while the dilute H2O2 with pure water show a weakest CL emission. The detailed and precise CL intensity difference induced by these solutions is further verifiably compared under the CL analysis system with the same H2O2, CPPO and CDs concentration (Fig. S4). As illustrated in Figs. S5 and 2e, the CL signal induced by H2O2/EtOH solution exhibit the highest CL intensity, which is 100-fold higher than the CL induced by H2O2/ H2O solution. Besides, the CL spectra in different polar solvents are also compared. In these CL reactions, the CL induced by CPPO-H2O2 system in high-polar solutions like water, DMF and DMAC exhibit a slight red shift compared with those relatively low-polar solutions like MeOH, EtOH and THF, which is similar to the solvent polarity induced change in their PL owing to the dipole − dipole and hydrogen bonding interactions (Fig. 2f). On the condition, the CL property of CDs is further investigated with H2O2/EtOH solution. As shown in Fig. 2g, the CDs exhibit green CL emission with a similar full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 89.5 nm with changing the adding order of the CL reaction sources. However, the CL intensity and efficiency of CDs in H2O2/EtOH solution are dramatically influenced by the order of addition. In these reactions, the order by adding H2O2/EtOH solution into the mixture of CPPO and CDs presents brightest and long-lasting CL emission (Fig. S6). These results indicate that CPPO is relatively unstable in EtOH, with most of it decomposing to imidazole before it can react with H2O2 [46,47] . Moreover, the CL reaction exhibit time-independent spectra, in which the CL wavelength centered at 530 nm has no change in the CL reaction process except the strength reduction (Fig. 2h). The relationship between the CL intensity and the concentration of three reactants are also probed. As illustration in Fig. 2i and S7, the CL signals of CDs exhibit a good linear relationship between the CL intensity and the concentration of three reactants, implying their potential application in CL analysis. In this work, green fluorescence of these CDs is believed to come from the intermediate band gap associated with the surface N-O state. In the CL process, the electron exchange between the CDs and energetic intermediate (1,2-dioxetanedione) can result in the excitation of lumiphores. Then, the chem-excited electron migrates from the crystalline core to surface groups and generate a radiative recombination with a bright green CL emission. To better clarify the origin of the intermediate states from which the radiative emission occurs, the CL emission have been measured with different metal ions in the CL systems. And it is found the CL intensity is strongly quenching by transition metal ions, especially Fe+3 ions as illustrated in Fig. S8a and S8b. On the basis of the comparison with the previous literatures, the surface states associated with nitrogen- or oxygen-related groups dominate the optical emission of the CDs [48–50] . Introduced transition metal ions can serve as electron exchange centre to seize the chem-excitation electronic charge from the surface of the CDs and finally generate a non-radiative electron–hole recombination which brings the system back to the ground state. The strong quenching effect of the CD based CL makes it possible to detect the transition metal ion Fe3+. As a proof of principle, different concentration of Fe3+ ions have been injected into the flow CL cell. As shown in the Fig. S8c and S8d, the CD signals illustrate a linear decrease with increasing the concentration of Fe3+ from 0 to 480 μm. And the limit of detection of the CL probe for Fe3+ is estimated to be 30.89 μm. So, the relevant results verify the CD based CL can be used in metal sensing applications.

More detailed experiments have been carried out to probe the mechanism behind water induced CL quenching. As shown in Fig. 3 a, when the extra water is added into the H2O2/EtOH solution, the CL emission from CPPO-H2O2 system can be seriously quenched with a reduction of ~ 75% in the CL integral intensity. Meanwhile, the time-dependent CL intensity recorded at the peak wavelength of 530 nm with and without extra water added in H2O2/EtOH solution is compared. It can be found the CL triggered by H2O2/EtOH solution exhibit further 23-times higher maximal CL intensity than those with adding extra water (Fig. 3b). With the CL analysis system, the relationship between the CL intensity and solvents with variable proportions of water are further investigated. As shown in Fig. 3c, the maximal CL intensity of CDs decrease exponentially with the increased ratio of water, indicating the water-triggered CL quenching. Thereby, detailed characterization of the CDs triggered by water has been investigated. As shown in Fig. 3d, the TEM image by depositing the CDs water drops on the carbon-coated copper grids are surveyed as similar as by depositing CDs ethanol drops. In the TEM image, it can be found the CDs have coupled and self-assembled into large CD clusters, implying the aggregation of CDs in aqueous. The assembly of surface charge-confined CDs through possible electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding [51–53] . The dynamic light scattering (DLS) of CDs in aqueous solution exhibits the distribution of single CD particles in the range of 1 to 10 nm and aggregated CD clusters in the range of 100 to 500 nm, corresponding to the investigation of TEM images (Fig. 3e). Moreover, the time-resolved spectra of the CDs dispersed in different volume ratio of water and ethanol present a gradually decreased PL lifetime with increasing the additive proportion of water (Fig. 3f). Thereby, it can be deduced that the CL quenching may stem from the aggregation triggered by water. As previous reports, the luminescence of the CDs in peroxalate-H2O2 system can be attributed to the chemically initiated electron exchange luminescence (CIEEL) between the CDs and energetic intermediate (1,2-dioxetanedione) produced in the chemical reaction. In the process, the electron exchange between the CDs and intermediates results in the excitation of lumiphores. And then, the excited CDs do-excite by irradiation, resulting in the CL emission (Fig. 3g). Thus, the CL quenching triggered by water can be deduced as illustrated in Fig. 3h. When the CDs are dispersed in organic solvents (such as ethanol), there are few CDs to aggregate, meaning low electron exchange or ET between CDs due to their large spatial distance. However, when the CDs are dispersed in water, the CDs can connect with each other and form self-assembled clusters due to the surface ionization induced by water. Hence, the electron exchange or ET between different CDs increases dramatically. Thus, the electron exchange between CDs and intermediate are seriously hindered, slashing the CL emission.

As an important means of regulation, surface chemical engineering has been employed to decrease the solvent effect and further improve the CL performance of CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, three kinds of common surfactants (CTAB, ODA and OA) are used to modify the CDs with different chemical grafting routes (Fig. S9). With different ligand molecules, the CDs modified with long-chains can be well-dispersed in dichloromethane (DCM) with a good hydrophobic property (Fig. 4b). The FT-IR spectra of three kinds of CDs after modification exhibit the vibration of CH2 , clearly indicating the existence of surface long-chain groups (Fig. 4c). Meanwhile, three kinds of CDs exhibit similar absorption band at ultraviolet and visible areas, indicating the similar PL principle with the original CDs (Fig. 4d). Moreover, three kinds of CDs still can emit similar bright green PL emission under 365-nm excitation as initial CDs (Fig. S10). With these modified CDs as emitter in the CL reaction, three kinds of CDs exhibit similar steady-state CL emission with a slight red-shift of 21, 2 and 10 nm after adding the H2O2/EtOH solution into the mixture of CPPO and CDs (Fig. S11). The CL spectrum and intensity decay after adding H2O2/EtOH solution have been compared, and it can be found three kinds of CDs exhibit shorter CL lifetime than that of the unmodified CDs but similar CL spectrum and maximal CL intensity (Fig. 4e and 4f). However, when the solvent for dispersing H2O2 is changed to water, the CDs modified with ODA exhibit far better CL performance than the initial CDs and the CDs modified with CTAB and OA in CL lifetime and maximal CL intensity (Fig. 4g). According to the CL spectrum and decay curves, the CL QYs triggered by H2O2/H2O have been calculated with an improved value from 1.32 × 10–4 to 1.15 × 10–3 einsteins mol−1 for ODA grafted CDs in comparison to the original CDs in water (Fig. 4h). These results imply the importance of the surface ligands in the process of CL and clearly reveal the surface chemical engineering can decrease the influence of water.

Although the long-chain ligands grafted on the surface of CDs can effectively improve the water-induced CL quenching in pure water media or due to the generated water from the CL chemical reaction, the overall performance of chemiluminescent CDs is still to be improved. In addition to the type of organic chain the length of chain on the surface of CDs may also influence the electron exchange between the CDs and intermediate through changing the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy level of CDs. Thereby, different amino ligands with variable chain length NBA, NTA and PbN are used to modify the CDs as illustrated in Fig. 5 a. As shown in Fig. 5b, the CDs grafted with different length of amino surfactants can be well-dispersed in DCM and exhibit a good hydrophobicity as similar as the CDs with ODA on the surface. The FT-IR spectra reveal the characteristic vibration of CH2 at 2950 and 2850 cm−1, indicating the existence of oleophylic ligands on the surface of CDs (Fig. 5c). Moreover, the three kinds of CDs present slightly red-shifting in absorption band tail at ultraviolet and visible areas with the decrease of chain length, which decreases the band gap of CDs and elevates the HOMO energy levels of CDs (Fig. 5d). In the cases, the modified CDs dispersed in EAC as fluorophores exhibit similar PL and steady-state CL emission, which is slightly red-shifted compared with the initial CDs due to the reduction of emission band gap (Figs. S12, S13 and 5e). The CL decay curves after adding H2O2/EtOH solution have been shown in Fig. 5f. As the chain length decreases, the CL lifetime of CDs dramatically improves from 0.61 to 24.3 s with a similar maximal CL intensity. And the CL QY of three kinds of CDs has been calculated according to the CL decay spectra (Fig. 5f and 5 g). As shown in Fig. 5g, the CDs capping with different long-chain ligands with a polar group (the amino group) on the tail of the alkyl chain demonstrate varying CL QYs. Among these CDs, the CDs modified with NBA illustrate excellent CL performance with a CL QY of 1.115 × 10–2 einsteins mol−1, which is about 5-fold higher than the initial CDs and comparable with RhB (1.155 × 10–2 einsteins mol−1, Figs. S14 and S15, Table 1 ). Moreover, it is found the CL QYs of CDs exhibit inversely proportional to the length of chain, which can be attributed to that the use of shorter chain length ligands in CDs has been an effective strategy to lift the HOMO energy levels of CDs as well as reduce the carrier injection barrier between the CDs and energetic intermediate (1,2-dioxetanedione) produced in the peroxyoxalate-H2O2 system (Fig. 5h). Therefore, it is demonstrated a potential benefit of surface chemical engineering for the improvement of CL performance of CDs.

With the efficient CL emission, the CDs modified with NBA (CDs@NBA) are attempted to be used as chemiluminescent emitter in cold light source for illumination or warning signs. As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, the DPB solution containing CPPO and CDs@NBA can emit bright and persistent (30-minutes visible to the naked eye) green CL emission after adding H2O2/TBA solution and the CL intensity is comparable with RhB. With a luminance meter, the illumination triggered by the CDs modified with different long-chain amino surfactants illuminations is recorded. As shown in Fig. 6c and d, the maximal CL luminance decreases with the increased length of surface chains in CDs. Moreover, the CDs@NBA (3 mg mL−1) exhibits the best maximal CL luminance (5.11 cd m−2) and longest lifetime (443 s) amongst all the CDs. In these chemiluminescent emitters, the maximal CL intensity of 3-mg mL−1 CDs@NBA is about 3-fold higher than 1-mg mL−1 commercially available RhB. And the integrated luminance intensity representing the whole photon emission in the CL process is 1.5-fold higher than the RhB on the same condition. The surface chemical engineering provides a promising route to achieve efficient CL used CDs as chemiluminescent emitter and develop potential applications in illuminationor bioimaging in the future.

In summary, green fluorescent CDs have been used as fluorophore in the chemical reaction of CPPO and H2O2 to emit green CL. It is found the CL of the CDs can be seriously quenched in water or by the produced water from the CL chemical reaction. The mechanism of the quenching can be ascribed to that the water can prevent the electron exchange between the CD and energetic intermediate produced in the CL reaction due to the excess ET or charge transfer between different CDs. With the surface chemical engineering, the coupling interaction between CDs and carrier injection barrier between the CDs and energetic intermediate can be effectively reduced. Thus, the CDs modified with the shortest alkyl chain (NBA) exhibit a maximal CL QY of 1.15 × 10–2 einsteins mol−1 and luminance of 5.11 cd m−2, which is about 5- and 13-fold higher than those of the initial CDs, and comparable with commercially available RhB. This work indicates the importance of the surface chemical modification in the process of CL emission and paves a facile route to achieve efficient chemiluminescent CDs.

Cheng-Long Shen: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Jin-Hao Zang: Formal analysis, Validation, Methodology. Qing Lou: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing - review & editing. Guang-Song Zheng: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Validation, Methodology. Meng-Yuan Wu: Formal analysis, Validation. Yang-Li Ye: Formal analysis, Validation. Jin-Yang Zhu: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Kai-Kai Liu: Writing - review & editing. Lin Dong: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Chong-Xin Shan: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Software, Project administration, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2021.149947.

ce:section.ce:para:
